perturbation.original_text: Commerce One shall provide Corio with support described in EXHIBIT C hereto, and maintenance in the form of Updates and Upgrades. Corio shall be responsible for providing its Customers with routine technical support of the Software and MarketSite.net Service. Corio shall escalate any technical support questions or problems it is unable to answer or resolve directly to Commerce One for Commerce One's immediate attention and resolution under the schedule set forth in EXHIBIT C hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Commerce One shall endeavor to provide Corio with support as generally described in EXHIBIT C hereto, and maintenance in the form of Updates and Upgrades. Corio should attempt to provide its Customers with routine technical support of the Software and MarketSite.net Service. Corio may escalate any technical support questions or problems it is unable to answer or resolve directly to Commerce One for Commerce One's consideration and possible resolution under the general guidelines set forth in EXHIBIT C hereto.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation for Commerce One to provide support to Corio and for Corio to support its customers. By replacing 'shall provide' with 'shall endeavor to provide' and 'shall be responsible' with 'should attempt to provide', the obligation becomes discretionary. Similarly, the replacement of 'Commerce One's immediate attention and resolution' with 'Commerce One's consideration and possible resolution' further diminishes the mandatory nature of the support.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text makes the provision of support and maintenance vague and discretionary, potentially contradicting the CLRA, which prohibits representing that goods or services have characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities which they do not have, or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection which the person does not have. By weakening the guaranteed level of support, the contract could be misrepresenting the characteristics of the service being provided, if the customers are made to believe they will get a higher level of support.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1770 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language weakens the obligation of Commerce One to provide support, and Corio to support its customers, creating a potential misrepresentation of the service characteristics. The original language uses 'shall provide' and 'shall be responsible,' indicating a firm commitment. The modified language uses 'shall endeavor to provide' and 'should attempt to provide,' which weakens this commitment. This directly relates to snippet 1, CIV 1770 (a)(5), which prohibits 'Representing that goods or services have...characteristics...that they do not have'. If customers are led to believe they will receive a high level of support, but the contract only obligates Commerce One and Corio to 'endeavor' or 'attempt' to provide support, this could be construed as a misrepresentation, hence a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Commerce One warrants that the Software and MarketSite.net Service will perform in substantial accordance with the Documentation, and the media on which the Software is distributed will be free from defects in materials and workmanship under normal use, for a period of sixty (60) days from the Effective Date, but in no event not later than December 31, 1999 (the "Warranty Period"). In addition, Commerce One warrants that during the Warranty Period the Software and MarketSite.net Service is free of any willfully introduced computer virus, or any other similar harmful, malicious or hidden program or data, which is designed to disable, erase, or alter the Software, or any other files, data, or software.
perturbation.changed_text: Commerce One generally warrants that the Software and MarketSite.net Service will potentially perform in accordance with some aspects of the Documentation, and the media on which the Software is distributed will hopefully be free from significant defects in materials and workmanship under typical use, for approximately sixty (60) days from the Effective Date, but in no event not definitively later than December 31, 1999 (the "Warranty Period"). In addition, Commerce One hopes that during the Warranty Period the Software and MarketSite.net Service is reasonably free of any intentionally introduced computer virus, or any other somewhat similar harmful, malicious or hidden program or data, which could be designed to disable, erase, or alter the Software, or any other files, data, or software.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces several ambiguities by using vague qualifiers like 'generally warrants,' 'potentially perform,' 'some aspects,' 'hopefully be free from significant defects,' 'typical use,' 'approximately sixty (60) days,' 'not definitively later than,' 'hopes that during the Warranty Period the Software and MarketSite.net Service is reasonably free of', 'intentionally introduced computer virus,' and 'somewhat similar harmful.' These phrases replace clear and definitive language with uncertain terms, weakening the warranty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC's implied warranty of merchantability ensures that goods are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. By introducing ambiguous and subjective language into the warranty, the modified text weakens the guarantee that the software and service will perform as expected. Terms like 'potentially perform' and 'some aspects' suggest the software might not consistently meet basic performance standards, contradicting the UCC's requirement of reasonable fitness for purpose.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces several ambiguities that weaken the warranty provided by Commerce One. According to the Law Explanation, the UCC's implied warranty of merchantability ensures that goods are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. By using ambiguous language, the changed text suggests the software might not consistently meet basic performance standards. The phrase 'potentially perform' contradicts the UCC's requirement of reasonable fitness for purpose, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-314 (2)(c), states that goods to be merchantable must be 'fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used.' The original text provides a clearer warranty, while the changed text weakens this guarantee through vague qualifiers, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any disputes arising out of this Agreement shall be resolved by binding arbitration in accordance with the then-current commercial arbitration rules of the American Arbitration Association ("RULES"). The arbitration shall be conducted by one (1) arbitrator appointed in accordance with the Rules in San Francisco County, California. A judgment upon the award may be entered in any court having jurisdiction of the parties, including without limitation the courts in San Francisco, California. The non-prevailing party in the arbitration shall pay all fees and charges of the American Arbitration Association; each party, however, shall be responsible for the payment of all fees and expenses connected with the presentation of its respective case.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes arising out of this Agreement may be addressed through non-binding arbitration, potentially following the general guidelines of commercial arbitration, perhaps as suggested by the American Arbitration Association ("RULES"). The arbitration could be conducted by an arbitrator vaguely associated with the Rules, potentially somewhere near San Francisco County, California. A judgment upon the potential award might be considered by a court arguably having some jurisdiction over the matter, possibly including courts near San Francisco, California. The 'non-prevailing' party may be 'encouraged' to contribute to some of the fees of the American Arbitration Association; each party, however, remains primarily responsible for their own costs.
perturbation.explanation: The revised text introduces multiple layers of ambiguity by replacing definitive terms with vague alternatives: 'shall be resolved' becomes 'may be addressed', 'binding arbitration' transforms into 'non-binding arbitration', 'in accordance with' changes to 'potentially following the general guidelines of', 'appointed in accordance with' is now 'vaguely associated with', and 'San Francisco County, California' becomes 'potentially somewhere near San Francisco County, California'. Furthermore, the mandatory payment of fees by the non-prevailing party is softened to 'may be encouraged to contribute', creating uncertainty about cost allocation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA outlines the grounds for vacating an arbitration award. By making the arbitration process non-binding and introducing significant ambiguity into the selection of the arbitrator and the enforcement of any potential award, the modified clause undermines the fundamental principles of arbitration as envisioned by the FAA. Section 10 describes conditions where the arbitration award may be vacated. The vague nature of this altered clause could open the door for challenges to the award's legitimacy, essentially nullifying the purpose of arbitration as a swift and decisive method of conflict resolution. This contradicts the FAA's intent to provide a clear and enforceable framework for arbitration agreements.

Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration- (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon s
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of the arbitration agreement, which contradicts the intent of the FAA. By changing 'shall be resolved by binding arbitration' to 'may be addressed through non-binding arbitration', the modified text makes the entire arbitration process optional and not binding. This directly contradicts the purpose of arbitration which is to resolve disputes swiftly and decisively. Scraped Law Snippet 1 outlines grounds for vacating an arbitration award under 9 USC 10, implying that arbitration awards are intended to be binding and enforceable. The changes to the arbitration clause introduces vagueness in arbitrator selection and enforcement, making it harder to uphold the award, thus creating a legal contradiction.
